evals.prompt: Use $change-explainer on my unstaged changes. I barely know this repository, so teach me what area I am looking at before you explain the diff. Give me one concrete before/after example and define the project terms you need.
evals.expected_output: The skill inspects local uncommitted changes plus the minimum README, architecture, entry-point, and neighboring code needed to teach a cold reader. It writes and opens a self-contained .change-explainer/<slug>.html with a self-ignoring .change-explainer/.gitignore. The visible beginner layer contains Start Here (whole/area/change context, a realistic Before/Now/Result story, and visible definitions for recurring project terms), What Changed (plain-language takeaway and a role-first diagram when it teaches a relationship), closed summaries for one to four core ideas, and Before/Now/Because recall anchors. Paths appear later in a role-based Where to Look table; migration and test breadth appear in Reviewer & Maintenance Details rather than as equal-weight beginner concepts.

evals.prompt: Build an HTML walkthrough of the last commit like I am reviewing this project cold. First tell me what the project and changed subsystem do. Then show a real scenario that failed or behaved differently before, what happens now, and only then teach the mechanism.
evals.expected_output: A self-contained beginner-first HTML lesson for the last commit, opened under .change-explainer/. Its first screen supplies enough project and subsystem baseline to understand the change, earns every specialized term before using it in a heading or diagram, and uses one concrete scenario as the learning spine. The overview and closed summaries are understandable without file paths. Expanded blocks progressively reveal one to four mechanisms with role-first visuals where useful; a later Where to Look table and maintainer section carry source anchors, invariants, compatibility, and proof.

evals.prompt: I pasted a PR patch below. Explain it as a browsable doc for someone who knows programming but not our architecture or domain. Group related hunks, keep the main story small, and stay descriptive unless something is materially risky.
evals.expected_output: A self-contained interactive HTML doc grouped by behavior rather than file. It reconstructs the smallest useful baseline from available context, defines domain and architecture vocabulary visibly, explains a concrete old-versus-new scenario, and promotes only one to four behavior-changing ideas. Supporting mechanical, documentation, migration, and test hunks remain accounted for in Where to Look or Reviewer & Maintenance Details. A Concerns section appears only for a material issue, with no formal-review language otherwise.

The skill defaults to the current repository's uncommitted change set, inspecting git status, staged and unstaged diffs, and relevant untracked files before asking for a target. It then follows the cold-reader scaffold by default and produces, opens, and reports a beginner-first HTML lesson under .change-explainer/. It asks for a commit, range, patch, or file pair only when the working tree has nothing to explain.

evals.prompt: Use $change-explainer on this commit. I own this subsystem, so keep the project primer very short and focus on the contract and maintenance consequences.
evals.expected_output: The explicit expert background shortens the project baseline and omits a vocabulary list when ordinary inline definitions suffice. The doc still states the old and new mental models and practical consequence before implementation detail, then emphasizes contract changes, code entry points, compatibility, tests, tradeoffs, and future-edit rules. It does not force a long beginner primer or decorative visuals onto an expert-oriented run.

evals.prompt: Use $change-explainer on this one-line validation fix. I am new to the project, but please do not inflate a tiny change. The request handler changed from `return save(request)` to `if (!request.id) throw new Error("id required"); return save(request)`.
evals.expected_output: A compact beginner-first HTML doc that gives only the baseline needed to understand the validation rule, one concrete Before/Now/Result example, and a single core-idea block. It omits the overview diagram when no meaningful relationship needs drawing, keeps vocabulary and maintainer detail minimal, and still provides the relevant source location and any material compatibility consequence.

evals.prompt: Compare these two versions of a policy document with $change-explainer. Assume I have never read the policy and teach the changed meaning before listing edits. Old §4: `Expense reports submitted after 17:00 are reviewed the next business day.` New §4: `Expense reports submitted at or after 16:00 are reviewed the next business day, except travel-emergency reports.`
evals.expected_output: A beginner-first HTML lesson that states the document's purpose, locates the changed section, gives a concrete old-versus-new policy scenario, defines policy terms, and groups changes by meaning. Where to Look points to sections or pages rather than pretending they are code paths; implementation-only language and visuals are omitted when they add no value.
